narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 96% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with strong indicators of bar passage to maintain high success rates. For you, a Black male with a middle-income family background, the model's decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, both at the bottom of the class—far below the typical student's mid-range standing around the 5th or 6th decile. These low rankings were the strongest signals pushing the prediction toward failure, suggesting to the model that early and later academic struggles could carry over to bar preparation challenges. Your LSAT score of 25, significantly lower than the average of about 37, was the second-biggest factor reinforcing this risk, as it stood out as a key predictor of exam readiness compared to most applicants. Even your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level, added further weight toward the failure prediction, though slightly less than the others. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 is below the typical 3.2 average, and your plan to attend full-time aligns with the vast majority of students, these played smaller roles overshadowed by the top concerns. In contrast to peers who typically pass with higher deciles and LSATs, your profile clusters with those the model sees as higher-risk for bar failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many from similar backgrounds overcome odds—but the model focuses on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours.
